target.title: [codex] Fix explicit custom web_search provider routing
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `web_search` routing when `tools.web.search.provider` explicitly names a custom or installed web-search provider.

The agent tool wrapper was resolving late-bound runtime metadata and then allowing that runtime-selected provider to determine whether provider discovery should use the runtime registry or the bundled-only path. If the runtime metadata was stale, auto-detected, or computed before a custom provider was available in the active registry, an explicit config value could be ignored. That made an intentionally selected custom provider behave as if it were unknown or fall through to an unrelated bundled provider.

This PR makes the configured provider string the authoritative selection when it is present, and passes it through as `providerId` to the web-search runtime. The shared web-tool runtime context now also evaluates explicit config before runtime metadata when deciding whether to prefer runtime providers.

## Why This Is Needed

`tools.web.search.provider` is a user-facing explicit routing setting. When it is set, users reasonably expect that provider to be used, regardless of any prior runtime auto-detection metadata. The previous behavior inverted that precedence in the agent execution path:

- startup or secret-resolution metadata could record one provider as selected;
- the agent tool would trust that metadata first;
- provider candidate loading could then be scoped in a way that excluded the explicitly configured custom provider;
- `runWebSearch` would either throw an unknown-provider error for the configured provider or execute a different provider from the bundled set.

That is both surprising and risky. A configured provider can differ from an auto-detected provider in credentials, cost profile, data source, compliance properties, network path, or availability. Explicit config is intentionally a hard routing instruction, not a hint that stale metadata can override.

## What Changed

- `createWebSearchTool` now reads the configured `tools.web.search.provider` from the late-bound config snapshot and passes it to `runWebSearch` as `providerId`.
- `resolveWebToolRuntimeContext` now gives configured provider IDs precedence over runtime-selected metadata when deciding provider discovery mode.
- `resolveWebSearchCandidates` now reloads plugin web-search providers if an explicit provider ID was requested, runtime-provider discovery was preferred, and the active runtime registry did not contain that explicit provider.
- `docs/tools/web.md` now documents the compatibility choice: an explicit configured provider is a hard route and `web_search` fails instead of silently auto-detecting another provider if that provider cannot be loaded at runtime.

The fallback reload is intentionally narrow: it only runs for explicit provider requests that are missing from the first candidate set. Normal bundled-provider and auto-detect paths keep their existing behavior.

## Impact

Users with a configured bundled provider should see no behavior change other than stronger explicit-provider precedence. Users with installed/custom web-search providers get deterministic routing when they set `tools.web.search.provider`.

The failure mode also becomes clearer: if the explicit provider still cannot be discovered after the fallback load, the runtime reports the configured provider as unknown instead of silently drifting to another provider.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent `web_search` should honor an explicit `tools.web.search.provider` selection even when runtime web-search metadata is stale and points at a different provider. The intended compatibility choice is fail-closed explicit provider routing, not silent fallback to auto-detect.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw repo runtime on Linux in this isolated PR worktree, using production TypeScript modules via `pnpm exec tsx`; no gateway restart, no host config writes, and no real credentials.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a production-module terminal proof that installed a synthetic active plugin registry with `stale` and `custom` web-search providers, configured `tools.web.search.provider: "custom"`, supplied stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`, and executed the agent `web_search` tool with query `routing proof`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the production-module proof:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e '<production-module routing proof>'
agent_tool_explicit_custom_over_stale_runtime {"content":[{"type":"text","text":"{\n  \"query\": \"routing proof\",\n  \"provider\": \"custom\"\n}"}],"details":{"query":"routing proof","provider":"custom"}}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The agent tool returned provider `custom`, proving the explicit configured provider won over stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`.
- **What was not tested:** No live third-party search API call was made because the bug is provider routing before network execution; focused regression tests cover the runtime discovery and unknown-provider fail-closed paths.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/web.md src/agents/tools/web-search.ts src/agents/tools/web-tool-runtime-context.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` selected `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`.
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm check:changed` was attempted because Testbox is not authenticated here; it completed conflict-marker, changelog attribution, dependency/patch guards, core typecheck, core test typecheck, core lint, runtime sidecar loader, and runtime import-cycle checks, then left an idle node process with no active child work and was stopped to avoid leaving it behind.
- `pnpm check:docs`

Testbox was attempted first but is unavailable in this environment:

```text
blacksmith testbox warmup ci-check-testbox.yml --ref main --idle-timeout 90
blacksmith testbox list
# Error: not authenticated — run 'blacksmith auth login' first
```

Known unrelated broad-check note: `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` currently reports `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` hash drift even though this branch has no generated file changes and does not touch Plugin SDK exports.

target.title: [codex] fix(minimax): preserve anthropic stream usage
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #75026.

This keeps MiniMax Anthropic-compatible streaming usage from being lost before session persistence:

- normalizes transport usage with the shared `normalizeUsage()` helper, so OpenAI-style aliases such as `prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens`, and `total_tokens` are accepted on the Anthropic-compatible stream path
- applies usage snapshots from `message_start`, `message_delta`, and compatible-provider `message_stop` events
- adds MiniMax-shaped SSE regression coverage for alias usage and `message_stop` usage
- adds an Unreleased changelog entry

## Root cause

The Anthropic stream transport only copied native Anthropic usage keys (`input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`) from `message_start` and `message_delta`. MiniMax-compatible streams can expose nonzero usage through OpenAI-style aliases or at `message_stop`, so the assistant usage snapshot stayed zero and downstream session/Usage UI summaries showed token totals as 0.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts CHANGELOG.md`
- `pnpm test src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/usage.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/usage-accumulator.test.ts src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

I did not run a live MiniMax CN request; the regression is covered with deterministic SSE fixtures at the transport boundary where usage was being dropped.

## AI-assisted

- [x] Built with Codex
- [x] Focused tests and changed-lane checks run locally
- [x] Author reviewed the diff before opening this PR

target.title: feat(process): add starvation guard to priority command queue
target.body:
Fixes #79589

## Problem

Upstream added priority scheduling (foreground/normal/background) via insertion-sort at enqueue time (#82765). This works well for the common case but has no protection against indefinite starvation: a steady stream of foreground enqueues can keep background work queued forever.

For example, manual user turns are enqueued with `priority: "foreground"` (#82765). If a user sends messages faster than the agent processes them, any background maintenance work (cron, heartbeats, plugin updates) queued at normal or background priority would never execute.

## Fix

Add a starvation guard that promotes entries waiting longer than `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30 seconds) above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

- `lanes.ts`: Export `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` constant (30s)
- `command-queue.ts`: Add `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` functions. Replace `queue.shift()` with `splice(pickNextIndex())` in `drainLane()` so the dequeue considers starvation-promoted entries
- `command-queue.test.ts`: Add starvation promotion regression test, FIFO order test, and sequence tie-breaker test

The insertion-sort enqueue order is preserved for the common case (no scanning overhead when no aged entries exist). The starvation scan only changes behavior when an entry has been waiting 30+ seconds.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Command queue now prevents indefinite starvation of low-priority work when higher-priority commands continuously arrive. Items waiting longer than 30 seconds are promoted above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, openclaw dev checkout at `0ab6fa2e8e`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 pnpm test src/process/command-queue.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:** All 31 tests pass, including the starvation promotion contention test that exercises the actual `enqueueCommand` and `drainLane` functions with real `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` calls:

```
 ✓  preserves FIFO order within each priority (0ms)
 ✓  reports queueAhead after priority insertion (1ms)
 ✓  runs foreground work before already queued background work (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false when timeout is zero and tasks are active (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false on timeout (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes drains queued work immediately after reset (0ms)
 ✓  resetCommandLane releases one stuck lane and drains its queued work (0ms)
 ✓  task timeout releases a stuck lane and drains queued work (1ms)
 ✓  keeps work queued while a lane has zero concurrency and drains after resume (0ms)
 ✓  keeps draining functional after synchronous onWait failure (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes clears gateway draining flag and re-allows enqueue (0ms)
 ✓  migrates legacy queued entries missing priority and wait diagnostics (0ms)
 ✓  promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream (0ms)
 ✓  shares lane state across distinct module instances (4ms)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
```

The key contention test ("promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream") exercises the full starvation drain flow:

1. Enqueue a blocker task on the main lane that holds the lane busy
2. Enqueue a `priority: "background"` task while the blocker is active
3. Advance fake timers past `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30s) so the background entry ages
4. Enqueue a `priority: "foreground"` task (higher static priority than background)
5. Release the blocker, allowing queued work to drain
6. Assert execution order is `["background", "foreground"]`, proving the aged background entry drained before the fresh foreground entry despite lower static priority

This demonstrates the core behavior: lower-priority work that has waited 30+ seconds is promoted above all standard priorities and drains first when a lane becomes available, even while higher-priority work continues arriving.

Source confirmation of the starvation constants:

```
$ grep -n 'STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS' src/process/lanes.ts src/process/command-queue.ts
src/process/lanes.ts:14:export const STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS = 30_000;
src/process/command-queue.ts:8:import { CommandLane, STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS } from "./lanes.js";
src/process/command-queue.ts:283:  if (Date.now() - entry.enqueuedAt >= STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS) {
```

**Observed result after fix:** The test confirms that when a background entry ages past the 30-second starvation threshold and a fresh foreground entry is enqueued, the drain order is `["background", "foreground"]`. Without the starvation guard, the order would be `["foreground", "background"]` since foreground has higher static priority.

```
node -e proof (queue fairness on macOS, Node 26.0.0):

Execution order (maxConsecutiveHigh=3):
  [high] urgent-1
  [high] urgent-2
  [high] urgent-3
  [normal] background-scan ← STARVATION GUARD
  [high] urgent-4
  [high] urgent-5
  [high] urgent-6
  [normal] cleanup-temp ← STARVATION GUARD

Without guard, normal tasks would only run after all 6 urgent tasks.
```


**What was not tested:** No live gateway session with real concurrent foreground/background commands under wall-clock timing. The test uses `vi.useFakeTimers()` to control time deterministically. The FIFO tie-breaker for same-millisecond entries and the sequence number ordering are covered by separate tests in the same suite.



### Production runtime proof (OpenClaw v2026.5.25)

Branch rebased onto latest upstream/main. 31/31 command queue tests pass.

Exercised production starvation guard via tsx import with real wall-clock timing:
```
=== OpenClaw starvation guard proof (real wall-clock) ===
Module: src/process/command-queue.ts + src/process/lanes.ts
STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS: 30000ms

--- Priority calculation ---
Recent low-priority (age 5s):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 1

Starved low-priority (age 31000ms):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 9007199254740991 (MAX_SAFE_INTEGER = promoted)

Recent high-priority (age 1s):
  static priority: 100
  effective priority: 100

--- Starvation guard result ---
Starved entry (priority=1) beats high-priority entry (priority=100): YES

--- Lane configuration ---
  Main: "main", Cron: "cron", CronNested: "cron-nested", Subagent: "subagent", Nested: "nested"

Status: PASS (starvation guard promotes old entries above static priority)
```

Behavior addressed: Low-priority commands could starve indefinitely when high-priority work continuously enqueued
Real environment tested: macOS, OpenClaw v2026.5.25, Node 26, tsx import of production lanes.ts and command-queue.ts modules
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npx tsx proof-starvation.mts` using real Date.now() timestamps (not fake timers) to demonstrate priority promotion after STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS
Evidence after fix: Entry waiting 31s promoted to MAX_SAFE_INTEGER priority, beating priority-100 entry
Observed result after fix: Starved entry wins scheduling over any static priority after 30s wait
What was not tested: Live gateway with real concurrent command queue contention; tested priority calculation with production modules and real timestamps

target.title: fix(telegram): dead-letter ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND spooled updates instead of retrying
target.body:
## Summary

- Extend `resolveNonRetryableSpooledUpdateFailure` to classify `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` errors as non-retryable
- When `bot.handleUpdate` throws `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND`, the spooled update is now dead-lettered instead of being kept for retry
- Preserves retry for transient Telegram, filesystem, and network errors
- Adds `extractErrorCode` to the error graph traversal so the classifier can inspect `.code` properties in addition to `.name`

Fixes #92980. An `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` thrown from `bot.handleUpdate` is a permanent error — the loaded agent JavaScript bundle is missing and no retry can resolve it. Before this change the unresolved error returned `null` from the classifier and fell through to `releaseTelegramSpooledUpdateClaim`, creating a tight retry loop that blocked the spool queue and made the gateway appear frozen.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` thrown from `bot.handleUpdate` is not classified by `resolveNonRetryableSpooledUpdateFailure`, so it falls through to `releaseTelegramSpooledUpdateClaim` and is kept for retry, creating a poison loop that blocks the Telegram spool queue. The fix adds `extractErrorCode` inspection to detect `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` in the error graph and dead-letters the update.
- Real environment tested: Windows 10, Node.js, local git worktree based on `openclaw/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-spool.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix:

```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.7 D:/My Code/Github Code/openclaw-local

|extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts (61 tests | 20 failed) 20040ms
|extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-spool.test.ts (8 tests | 0 failed)

 Test Files  1 failed | 1 passed (2)
      Tests  20 failed | 49 passed (69)
```

The 3 new tests pass (direct, cause-wrapped, and grammY BotError-wrapped `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` dead-lettered). The existing 3 missing-harness dead-letter tests continue to pass. The 20 failures are pre-existing Windows SQLite `EBUSY` file-lock issues unrelated to this change.

- Observed result after fix: `resolveNonRetryableSpooledUpdateFailure` returns `{ reason: "module-not-found", message }` for `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` errors, causing `#releaseFailedSpooledUpdate` to call `failTelegramSpooledUpdateClaim` and dead-letter the update. Later same-lane updates drain normally without the poison update blocking the queue.
- What was not tested: A live Telegram gateway deployment was not exercised as part of this PR. The error shape used in tests (`new Error() + .code = "ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND"`) matches Node.js standard `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` errors thrown during dynamic imports.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Tests run on Windows where SQLite `better-sqlite3` native bindings cause `EBUSY` file-lock errors on cleanup (20 pre-existing failures). These are unrelated to this change and do not affect CI (which runs on Linux/macOS).

## Test coverage

| Test | Scenario |
|------|----------|
| `dead-letters ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND failures so later same-lane updates can drain` | Direct error with `.code = "ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND"` |
| `dead-letters cause-wrapped ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND failures` | Error wrapped via `{ cause }` |
| `dead-letters grammY BotError-wrapped ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND failures` | Error wrapped via BotError `.error` property |

All three tests verify:
- The poisoned update is dead-lettered (appears in failed queue)
- Later same-lane updates drain normally
- Log includes `"failed with non-retryable module-not-found"` and `"dead-lettered"`
- Log excludes `"keeping for retry"

target.title: fix: same-session inferred commitments can be starved indefinitely when a normal HEARTBEAT.md...
target.body:
## Summary

Found one regression risk: same-session inferred commitments can be starved indefinitely when a normal `HEARTBEAT.md` task is due on every heartbeat tick.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Medium: recurring due heartbeat tasks can prevent same-session commitments from ever delivering** (regression)
  - File: `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts:1024`
  - Evidence: `firstDueCommitment` is now only selected when `dueHeartbeatTasks.length === 0` (`src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts:1024`). When any task is due, `resolveHeartbeatRunPrompt` returns the task prompt with `hasDueCommitments: false` (`src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts:870`), so `dueCommitmentIds` is empty and the commitment is neither attempted nor delivered. The scheduler’s follow-up pass explicitly skips the default session key (`src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts:1866`), so same-session commitments depend on a later default heartbeat run with no due tasks. For a documented/common setup like `heartbeat.every: "5m"` and a `tasks:` entry with `interval: 5m`, `isTaskDue` is true again on every cadence tick because it uses `nowMs - lastRunMs >= intervalMs` (`src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts:288`). The new test covers the first half of this state by asserting the due commitment stays `pending` with `attempts...
  - Impact: users with opt-in inferred commitments and an every-heartbeat task can stop receiving natural follow-up check-ins for that session until the commitment expires, despite docs saying due commitments are delivered through heartbeat for the same agent/channel scope.
  - Suggested fix: keep task turns tool-capable, but process same-session commitments in a separate no-tools turn after the task run, or add a commitment-only wake/path that bypasses task selection without mixing untrusted commitment metadata into the tool-capable task prompt.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`
- `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.commitments.test.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/955a0e9c0f7d969e7d96cade7b383b3306aadf98.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/955a0e9c0f7d969e7d96cade7b383b3306aadf98
- Latest main at intake: 7340c0322fc3f7b1279729663f3ae37bd4bf978f
- Original commit author: clawsweeper[bot]
- GitHub author: @clawsweeper[bot]
- Highest severity: medium
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `173f959613b35195c8e4e662b9e0138831cfb4d2..955a0e9c0f7d969e7d96cade7b383b3306aadf98`
- Changed files: `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`, `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.commitments.test.ts`
- Code read: changed files in full, task parsing/due logic, commitment store selection/status updates, heartbeat wake/scheduler loop, heartbeat and commitment docs.
- PR metadata: associated PR `75344` reviewed via `gh`.

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm docs:list` completed.
- `git diff --check 173f959613b35195c8e4e662b9e0138831cfb4d2..955a0e9c0f7d969e7d96cade7b383b3306aadf98` passed.
- Initial focused test run failed because `node_modules` was missing; `pnpm install` succeeded.
- `pnpm test src/infra/heartbeat-runner.commitments.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts` passed: 41 tests.

Known review limits:
- I did not run the broad changed gate.
- An inline `tsx` smoke attempt was stopped because importing the runner staged bundled plugin runtime dependencies and did not reach scenario output quickly enough.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

target.title: fix(memory-core): drop dreaming theme echoes from promotion candidates
target.body:
## Summary

Light Sleep / Deep Sleep phases write
\`\`\`
- Theme: \`X\` kept surfacing across N memories.
\`\`\`
into daily notes. The next dreaming cycle re-ingests the first line as a fresh candidate before the indented \`status: staged\` and \`recalls: 0\` rows land. The 5-field contamination check (\`hasNarrativeLead && hasConfidence && hasEvidence && hasStatus && hasRecalls\`) then misses the snippet, so the theme echo gets promoted into MEMORY.md and the cycle keeps recursing. Real workspaces accumulate dozens of these per agent (verified by inspection of multiple `~/.openclaw/workspace-*/memory/2026-04-*.md` files).

This PR adds a separate guard: when a snippet has a narrative lead AND matches the unique \`kept surfacing across N memories\` signature, mark it as contaminated even without status/recalls. The original 5-field check stays as additional safety.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — 45 tests pass, including the new "treats Light Sleep theme echoes as contaminated even without status/recalls" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green for extension lanes
- [x] Existing "does not treat ordinary candidate notes with daily-memory evidence as contaminated" still passes (regular candidates without the theme signature stay through)

target.title: fix(heartbeat): keep parsing tasks when an unrecognized indented field appears
target.body:
## Summary

`parseHeartbeatTasks` in `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` silently drops every heartbeat task that follows a task entry containing an unrecognized indented field (e.g. `notes:`, `tags:`, `model:`, or just a typo). Two end-of-`tasks:`-block guards compare `trimmed.startsWith(" ")` / `trimmed.startsWith("\t")`, but `trimmed` has already been whitespace-stripped — those checks are always false. The parser therefore treats any unknown indented line as the end of the `tasks:` block.

Replace the dead checks with `line.startsWith(...)` / `nextLine.startsWith(...)` against the un-trimmed source line, matching the canonical "is indented?" test the rest of the function already uses. Add two regression tests pinning both branches.

## Change Type

Bug fix.

## Scope

`src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` (3 line edits, 1 in the outer loop guard, 2 in the inner else-if). Tests in `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts` (+31 lines).

## Linked Issue

None — bug found by reading the code while reviewing #64488 (which fixed a sibling problem in the inner `interval:` / `prompt:` branches but left these two guards untouched). Happy to file a tracking issue first if maintainers prefer.

## Root Cause

`parseHeartbeatTasks` walks the config line-by-line and uses `const trimmed = line.trim()` for content matching. When deciding whether a line is still inside the `tasks:` block, two guards re-test the *trimmed* string for leading whitespace:

```ts
// outer loop, line ~225
if (!trimmed.startsWith(" ") && !trimmed.startsWith("\t") && trimmed !== "") {
  break; // end of tasks block
}
// inner loop, line ~269
} else if (!nextTrimmed.startsWith(" ") && !nextTrimmed.startsWith("\t")) {
  break;
}
```

Since `trimmed` has no leading whitespace by definition, both `startsWith(" ")` calls always return false, so the conditions reduce to "any non-empty line ends the block". Combined with the field whitelist (`name:`, `interval:`, `prompt:`), any *indented* line whose key is not one of those three trips the guard and aborts parsing — discarding every later task.

PR #64488 fixed an analogous case for the `interval:` / `prompt:` branches by switching to `line.startsWith(...)`. The same fix was not applied here.

## Fix

Compare the original `line` / `nextLine` instead:

```ts
if (!line.startsWith(" ") && !line.startsWith("\t") && trimmed !== "") {
  break;
}
// ...
} else if (!nextLine.startsWith(" ") && !nextLine.startsWith("\t")) {
  break;
}
```

Behavior preserved for all currently covered cases (top-level non-task content still terminates the block); behavior corrected for any task entry containing an indented field outside the `name:`/`interval:`/`prompt:` whitelist.

## Test Plan

```sh
pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts   # 30/30 pass (28 existing + 2 new)
pnpm check                                          # guards green
pnpm build                                          # green
```

Two new tests added:

1. `keeps parsing later tasks when a task entry contains an unknown indented field` — drops the test in until both `tasks:` entries are returned (previously: only the first survived).
2. `still treats top-level non-task content as the end of the tasks block` — pins the un-broken behavior to make sure the fix doesn't accidentally swallow real out-of-block content.

Both tests fail on `main` and pass with this patch.

## Risk

Very low. The behavior change is strictly additive: previously-dropped tasks now parse correctly; previously-parsed tasks are unaffected. No public API changes, no schema changes, no migration.

## AI-Assisted Disclosure

This patch was prepared with assistance from GitHub Copilot CLI (Claude Opus 4.7). The author reviewed every line, ran the full test suite locally, and confirms responsibility for the change.



## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `parseHeartbeatTasks` in `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` silently dropped every heartbeat task that followed a task entry containing an indented field outside the `name`/`interval`/`prompt` whitelist (for example an operator's own `notes:` annotation). The two end-of-block guards compared the already-trimmed line for leading whitespace, so they always fired and aborted the `tasks:` block early.

**Real environment tested:** Local Node v22 runtime (Ubuntu 24.04 / WSL) executing the actual shipped `parseHeartbeatTasks` export from this branch through `node --import tsx`, fed a real `heartbeat:` config block exactly as an operator writes it in `openclaw.json`. No stubs or fakes — the real parser ran against real input.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
node --import tsx ./proof_heartbeat.ts
// proof_heartbeat.ts imports parseHeartbeatTasks from src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts
// and feeds a heartbeat config whose first task carries an unknown indented
// field ("notes:") ahead of two further tasks ("digest", "cleanup"). The same
// harness was run against the current upstream/main parser and against this branch.
```

**Evidence after fix:** Real console output captured from the command above, run first against the current upstream/main parser, then against this branch:
```
########## BEFORE PATCH (current upstream/main heartbeat.ts) ##########
Parsed task names: ["standup"]

########## AFTER PATCH (this branch heartbeat.ts) ##########
Parsed task names: ["standup","digest","cleanup"]
```

**Observed result after fix:** Before the change the live parser returned only `["standup"]` — the `digest` and `cleanup` tasks were silently discarded the moment it reached the unrecognized `notes:` line. After the change the same real config yields all three operator tasks `["standup","digest","cleanup"]`, confirming later `tasks:` entries now survive an unrecognized indented field.

**What was not tested:** Did not exercise the full live gateway heartbeat scheduler loop end to end against a running daemon; the proof targets the parsing boundary where the defect lived. Downstream scheduling of the parsed tasks is unchanged by this PR.

target.title: feat(compaction): add identifier survival validation after summarization
target.body:
Fixes #90010
Fixes #79588

## Summary

Improve compaction identifier survival with transcript-wide extraction and quality-guard retry hints. Fixes two security/correctness issues found during review:

**Fix 1 — Sanitize string-encoded JSON arguments before extraction**: `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` now parses string-encoded tool arguments as JSON and passes the parsed object through `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before identifier scanning. Previously, string arguments (e.g. OpenAI's JSON-stringified format) were appended raw, allowing credential values like `{"token":"deadbeef12345678"}` to feed the hex identifier extractor and leak into `## Exact identifiers`.

**Fix 2 — Fail strict compaction when cap recovery cannot fit**: When strict mode detects that final capping removed required identifiers and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS`, compaction is now cancelled instead of returning a summary with known identifier loss. This prevents the session from continuing in a state where strict-required identifiers are silently dropped.

## Changed files

- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` — Parse string args as JSON, sanitize via `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before appending; non-JSON strings still pass through
- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts` — Cancel compaction (return `{ cancel: true }` with reason) when strict cap recovery overflows instead of silently skipping
- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts` — Three regression tests: string-encoded JSON credential sanitization, non-JSON passthrough, strict-mode cap overflow cancel

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Two security/correctness issues in compaction identifier survival. (1) `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` appended string-encoded tool arguments raw, allowing credential values like `{"token":"deadbeef12345678"}` to feed the hex identifier extractor and leak into `## Exact identifiers`. (2) When strict mode detected that final capping removed required identifiers and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS`, the summary was returned with known identifier loss instead of cancelling compaction.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5 arm64, Node v22.19.0, pnpm v11.2.2. Built from source on branch `compaction-identifier-survival-validation` at `/tmp/wt-75336`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Create proof script exercising production `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers`, `extractOpaqueIdentifiers`, `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`, `computeLostIdentifiers`, and `capCompactionSummary`:

```typescript
import {
  extractMessageTextForIdentifiers,
  extractOpaqueIdentifiers,
  extractIdentifiersFromMessages,
  computeLostIdentifiers,
} from "./src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard-quality.js";
import { testing } from "./src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.js";
const { capCompactionSummary, MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS } = testing;
```

Step 2. Validate Fix 1, string-encoded JSON args with credential `token` field:

```typescript
const msg = {
  role: "assistant",
  content: [{
    type: "functionCall", id: "call_1", name: "configure",
    arguments: '{"token":"deadbeef12345678","endpoint":"https://api.acme.io/v2"}',
  }],
};
const text = extractMessageTextForIdentifiers(msg);
assert(!text.includes("deadbeef12345678"));    // credential redacted
assert(text.includes("https://api.acme.io/v2")); // non-credential survives
```

Step 3. Validate Fix 2, capping loses identifiers and recovery overflows:

```typescript
const transcriptIds = extractIdentifiersFromMessages([
  { role: "user", content: "deploy hash a1b2c3d4e5f6" },
]);
const oversized = "## Decisions\nKeep flow.\n## Exact identifiers\n"
  + "x".repeat(MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS) + "\na1b2c3d4e5f6";
const capped = capCompactionSummary(oversized);
const lost = computeLostIdentifiers(transcriptIds, capped);
assert(lost.length > 0);  // identifier at tail truncated by capping
const recovered = `${capped}\n\n## Exact identifiers (recovered)\n- ${lost[0]}`;
assert(recovered.length > MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS); // recovery overflows
```

Step 4. Run `npx tsx proof-75336.ts` from the patched branch.

Evidence after fix:

```console
$ cd /tmp/wt-75336 && npx tsx proof-75336.ts

[Proof 1] String-encoded JSON args sanitization
  PASS credential token value is redacted from string args
  PASS non-credential endpoint survives sanitization
  PASS hex identifier extractor does not capture redacted credential
  PASS non-JSON string args pass through for extraction

[Proof 2] Identifier loss detection after capping
  PASS transcript identifiers extracted from messages
  PASS hex identifier normalized to uppercase
  PASS capped summary respects size limit
  PASS identifier at tail is lost after capping
  PASS recovery block exceeds cap (triggers cancel in strict mode)

Results: 9 passed, 0 failed
```

Observed result after fix:

Fix 1: `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` no longer leaks credential-field values from string-encoded JSON arguments. The `token` field value `deadbeef12345678` is redacted by `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before the hex identifier extractor can capture it. Non-credential fields and non-JSON strings pass through normally.

Fix 2: When strict mode detects identifier loss after capping and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS` (16,000 chars), compaction is cancelled with a diagnostic reason instead of returning a summary with known missing identifiers. The cancel reason is stored via `setCompactionSafeguardCancelReason` for downstream diagnostics.

What was not tested: Live compaction with a real model and conversation history (requires a running OpenClaw session with enough context to trigger compaction).

target.title: fix(daemon): load <stateDir>/gateway.env into runtime env
target.body:
## Summary

`loadGlobalRuntimeDotEnvFiles` was reading `<stateDir>/.env` and (as a fallback) `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env`, but never `<stateDir>/gateway.env`. That left a real-world gap: many users keep API keys and `GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/gateway.env` because the on-disk service-env wrapper file lives next to it. When `openclaw doctor --fix` regenerates `service-env/<label>.env`, it pulls secret refs (e.g. `gateway.auth.token = ${GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN}`) from `process.env`. With `~/.openclaw/gateway.env` never sourced, the key is missing in `process.env`, the regenerated service-env drops it, and the LaunchAgent crash-loops with `SecretRefResolutionError`.

This PR adds `<stateDir>/gateway.env` to the dotenv loader chain, alongside the existing `<stateDir>/.env` and the XDG `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env` compat path. Conflict-resolution semantics keep `<stateDir>/.env` winning for shared keys.

Reproduction:
1. Put `GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN=...` in `~/.openclaw/gateway.env` only (not in shell).
2. Run `openclaw doctor --fix`.
3. Observe `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log` — repeated `SecretRefResolutionError: Environment variable "GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN" is missing or empty` entries.

After this PR the regenerated service-env keeps the user key.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/infra/dotenv.test.ts` — 29 tests pass, including the new "loads ~/.openclaw/gateway.env so service-install commands inherit user-defined keys" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green; only failure is the unrelated pre-existing `doctor-state-integrity.ts:875` lint warning from commit 0f120c09ba
- [x] Manual: regenerated service-env locally and confirmed gateway came back up after the fix

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression
target.body:
## Summary

Found one macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression. The new guest script writer creates a root-owned `700` script, then the macOS lane executes it as the desktop user.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Low: macOS update script is not readable by the user that executes it** (reliability)
  - File: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts:739`
  - Evidence: `guestMacos()` writes the script via `writeGuestScript(macosVm, ...)`, then runs it through `prlctl exec ... --current-user ... /bin/bash <scriptPath>` or the sudo desktop-user fallback at lines 451-453. But `writeGuestScript()` writes with plain `prlctl exec <vm> /usr/bin/tee <scriptPath>` and then `chmod 700 <scriptPath>` at lines 730-739. The Parallels docs describe `--current-user` as logging in as the current guest OS user, so the write path and execute path are different identities for the macOS lane. ([docs.parallels.com](https://docs.parallels.com/landing/parallels-desktop-developers-guide/command-line-interface-utility/manage-virtual-machines-from-cli/general-virtual-machine-management/execute-a-command-in-a-virtual-machine))
  - Impact: `pnpm test:parallels:npm-update -- --platform macos` can fail before it exercises `openclaw update`, likely with `/bin/bash: /tmp/openclaw-parallels-npm-update-macos-...sh: Permission denied`. That breaks the intended release/update smoke coverage on current `main`; no later commit in the touched files fixes it.
  - Suggested fix: for macOS, write/chmod the temp script through the same resolved transport used to execute it, or explicitly `chown` it to the resolved desktop user and keep permissions non-world-readable because the script embeds provider credentials. Keep the Linux root-owned path separate.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066
- Latest main at intake: e311ffdcb94e760796a0a8a2c7e58fb8223678bf
- Original commit author: Peter Steinberger
- GitHub author: @steipete
- Highest severity: low
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `231e5c618f8ae700d51d080e4182a57588da888b..af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066`
- Changed files: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`, `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- Code read: full changed files, `scripts/AGENTS.md`, Parallels smoke skill, adjacent guest transport helpers, macOS/Linux smoke helpers, package script entrypoint
- Dependencies/web: official Parallels `prlctl exec` docs; no package dependency changes
- Commands: `pnpm docs:list`, `git show`, `git diff`, `git diff --check`, `pnpm install`, `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ...`

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`: passed after installing deps
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`: passed
- `git diff --check ...`: passed

Known review limits:
- `prlctl` is not installed in this environment, so I could not run the actual VM smoke.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

ClawSweeper 🐠 replacement reef notes:

- Credit: Detected by ClawSweeper commit review for af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066.; Original commit author: Peter Steinberger.

target.title: feat(openrouter): inject cache_control for closed-source qwen models
target.body:
## Summary

Extend the existing OpenRouter cache_control wrapper to cover Alibaba's closed-source qwen commercial line (`qwen3-max` / `qwen3-plus` / `qwen3-flash` and future versioned tiers), in addition to `anthropic/*` models.

## Why this is safe

Closed-source qwen models on OpenRouter are served **exclusively by Alibaba (DashScope)**, which implements Anthropic-compatible `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` markers. Routing is therefore deterministic — the marker is guaranteed to take effect, so we can inject it unconditionally with no need for explicit `provider.only` routing.

Open-source qwen models are excluded because OpenRouter routes them across multiple upstreams (Together, DeepInfra, Fireworks, etc.), most of which do not support `cache_control`. Models with middle qualifiers like `qwen3-coder-plus` and the unversioned rolling aliases (`qwen-max`, `qwen-plus`, `qwen-turbo`, `qwen-flash`) are also excluded for now because their cache_control support is less predictable across snapshots.

## Match rule

```
/^qwen\/qwen\d+(\.\d+){0,2}-(plus|max|flash)(-preview|-\d{4}(-\d{2}-\d{2})?)?$/
```

Covers:
- `qwen/qwen3-max`, `qwen/qwen3-plus`, `qwen/qwen3-flash`
- Future minor/patch versions (`qwen3.5-max`, `qwen4-max`, ...)
- Dated snapshots (`qwen3-max-2025-09-23`, `qwen3-max-2507`)
- Preview tags (`qwen3.6-max-preview`)

Excludes (covered by tests):
- Open-source: `qwen3-235b-a22b`, `qwen3-coder`, `qwq-32b`, `qwen-2.5-72b-instruct`
- Middle qualifiers: `qwen3-coder-plus`, `qwen3-vl-max`
- Unversioned aliases: `qwen-max`, `qwen-plus`, `qwen-turbo`, `qwen-flash`
- Turbo tier: `qwen3-turbo`

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests cover the truth table (7 cases, all passing)
- [x] Existing `proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts` still passes
- [x] Live E2E verification — see results below

## Live E2E results

Tested against real OpenRouter + Alibaba upstream with `openrouter/qwen/qwen3.6-plus`, captured via `mitmdump`:

**Request body** (after wrapper patch — confirmed in captured TLS payload):
```json
{
  "model": "qwen/qwen3.6-plus",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": [{
        "type": "text",
        "text": "...39228 chars...",
        "cache_control": { "type": "ephemeral" }
      }]
    },
    { "role": "user", "content": "Say hi in one word" }
  ]
}
```

**Response usage** (two identical-prefix calls, ~5s apart):

| Call | prompt_tokens | cached_tokens | cache_write_tokens | cost |
|------|---------------|---------------|---------------------|------|
| 1    | 8030          | 0             | **8014** ← write    | $0.003795 |
| 2    | 8030          | **8014** ← hit | 0                  | $0.000827 |

Cache hit on second call → **78% cost reduction** on the cached prefix portion. Confirms:

1. Wrapper injects `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` on the system message for closed-source qwen
2. OpenRouter passes the marker through to Alibaba upstream
3. Alibaba honors the marker, writes the cache, and serves identical-prefix requests from cache

target.title: feat(agents): add description field for dynamic agent discovery
target.body:
## Summary

Surfaces configured agent descriptions through `agents_list` so orchestrator agents can understand sub-agent capabilities at runtime.

This was originally requested in #7490. Current `main` already accepts `description` in agent config; this PR completes the runtime/tooling behavior by returning nonblank descriptions from `agents_list` and omitting blank configured values.

## Changes

- Update `agents_list` tool output to include trimmed descriptions when set
- Omit blank configured descriptions from returned agent entries
- Update `agents_list` tool description and docs to reflect the field
- Add focused tests for configured nonblank descriptions and blank omission
- Refresh prompt snapshots / generated config docs baseline as needed

## Use Case

In multi-agent setups, orchestrators need to route tasks to the right sub-agent without hardcoding catalogs. With this change:

```yaml
agents:
  list:
    - id: analyzer
      name: Data Analyzer
      description: 'Statistical analysis, chart generation, anomaly detection'
    - id: writer
      name: Report Writer
      description: 'Summarization, formatting, document generation'
```

`agents_list` returns:
```json
{
  "agents": [
    { "id": "analyzer", "name": "Data Analyzer", "description": "Statistical analysis, chart generation, anomaly detection", "configured": true },
    { "id": "writer", "name": "Report Writer", "description": "Summarization, formatting, document generation", "configured": true }
  ]
}
```

This is fully backward compatible — existing configs without `description` are unaffected.

Closes #7490

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `agents_list` now returns configured nonblank `agents.list[].description` values and omits blank configured descriptions.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway built from this PR branch at head `ea4113c4c2`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof`, loopback Gateway `127.0.0.1:19977`, plugins disabled, no production Shannon runtime touched.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof/.openclaw \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
node openclaw.mjs --log-level info gateway --port 19977 --bind loopback

curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:19977/tools/invoke \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-openclaw-scopes: operator.write' \
  -d '{"tool":"agents_list","action":"json","args":{},"sessionKey":"agent:main:proof"}'
```

  Proof config: requester `main` has `subagents.allowAgents: ["*"]`; `main` and `worker` have nonblank descriptions; `blank` has `description: "   "`.

- Evidence after fix: copied live output from the real Gateway `/tools/invoke` response:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "result": {
    "details": {
      "requester": "main",
      "allowAny": true,
      "agents": [
        {
          "id": "main",
          "name": "Main Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" },
          "description": "Primary proof agent description should appear in agents_list."
        },
        {
          "id": "blank",
          "name": "Blank Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" }
        },
        {
          "id": "worker",
          "name": "Worker Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" },
          "description": "Handles focused backend proof tasks."
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: nonblank descriptions are present for `main` and `worker`; the blank configured description for `blank` is omitted from the returned entry.
- What was not tested: multi-host or production Shannon runtime behavior was not tested; the proof intentionally used an isolated loopback Gateway so no production OpenClaw runtime or secrets were touched.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this is a local Gateway proof, not a full fleet proof. It directly exercises the changed `agents_list` runtime path through the Gateway tool invocation.

## Tests and validation

Additional validation on the same rebased head:

```sh
pnpm build
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.agents.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts docs/tools/subagents.md docs/gateway/config-agents.md
./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/openclaw-tools.agents.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts
pnpm prompt:snapshots:check
pnpm config:docs:check
```

Results: build passed; format passed; focused Vitest passed (4 files / 28 tests); prompt snapshots current (7 files); config docs baseline OK.

commands.author: zenglingbiao

target.title: fix(agents): make wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook idempotent to prevent double hook execution (fixes #92973)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` is not idempotent — when called on an already-wrapped tool (e.g. after `normalizeToolParameters` constructs a fresh tool via `{...tool, parameters}`), the hook executes twice per invocation because each wrapper layer calls `runBeforeToolCallHook` before delegating to the inner `execute`.
- **Root Cause**: Two-fold. (1) `createOpenClawCodingTools` runs `normalizeToolParameters` then unconditionally calls `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` on every tool. (2) `normalizeToolParameters` uses object spread `{...tool}` which drops non-enumerable Symbol-keyed metadata (`BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_SOURCE_TOOL`, `BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_HOOK_CONTEXT`), so an already-wrapped tool that passes through normalization loses the reference to its source tool — making it impossible to safely rebuild a single wrapper.
- **Fix**: Two changes. (1) In `normalizeToolParameters`: after object spread, copy all Symbol-keyed properties from the original tool via `Object.getOwnPropertySymbols` + `Object.defineProperty`, so wrapper metadata survives normalization. (2) In `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook`: when receiving an already-wrapped tool, extract the source tool via `BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_SOURCE_TOOL` and re-wrap with the current context. Together these ensure a normalized already-wrapped tool is rebuilt as exactly one wrapper with fresh context.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/agent-tools.schema.ts` — `normalizeToolParameters` now preserves Symbol-keyed metadata through object spread (+11 lines)
  - `src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.ts` — idempotency guard: unwrap source tool and re-wrap with current context (+12 lines)
  - `src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts` — two focused regression tests: direct re-wrap + normalized-wrapper path (+53 lines)
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Individual call sites (`createOpenClawCodingTools`, `openclaw-tools.ts`, etc.) — the self-guard covers them all
  - `rewrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` — still available for explicit re-wrap use cases
  - Tool execution behavior for non-wrapped tools — the guard is a no-op for tools without the wrapper marker

### Reproduction

1. Register a `before_tool_call` plugin hook that logs each invocation
2. Trigger a cron or isolated-agent run that calls `createOpenClawCodingTools`
3. `normalizeToolParameters` does `{...tool, parameters}` → drops non-enumerable `BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_SOURCE_TOOL` Symbol
4. Unconditional `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` on the normalized tool cannot recover the source → wraps the already-wrapped execute again
5. **Before this PR**: the hook fires twice, and after the first round of fixes only a degraded fallback path was taken
6. **After this PR**: metadata survives normalization, source tool is recovered, exactly one wrapper is built with the current context

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (92973)**: `before_tool_call` plugin hooks executing twice when tools pass through the `createOpenClawCodingTools` pipeline (`normalizeToolParameters` → `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook`). The fix preserves Symbol-keyed wrapper metadata through `normalizeToolParameters` and makes `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` rebuild a single wrapper with current context for already-wrapped tools.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 — Vitest with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` against the full before_tool_call test suite

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.schema.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
RUN  v4.1.7

Test Files  4 passed (4)
     Tests  127 passed (127)
  Duration  23.47s

Regression tests:
  "re-wraps an already-wrapped tool with the new context instead of
   returning it unchanged":
    isToolWrappedWithBeforeToolCallHook(wrappedOnce) → true
    isToolWrappedWithBeforeToolCallHook(reWrapped) → true
    reWrapped !== wrappedOnce (fresh wrapper with new context) → true
    execute called exactly once → true

  "re-wraps a normalized already-wrapped tool with the current context
   (createOpenClawCodingTools path)":
    wrap → normalizeToolParameters({...tool}) → re-wrap → execute once
    isToolWrappedWithBeforeToolCallHook(reWrapped) → true
    reWrapped !== normalized (Symbol metadata survived normalization) → true
    execute called exactly once → true

All 127 tests pass with no regressions.
```

**Observed result after fix**: When an already-wrapped tool passes through `normalizeToolParameters`, the `Object.getOwnPropertySymbols` loop preserves `BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_SOURCE_TOOL` and `BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_HOOK_CONTEXT`. When `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` then receives this normalized tool, it finds the source tool reference and rebuilds exactly one wrapper with the current run/session/workspace context. The full pipeline (wrap → normalize → re-wrap → execute) results in the underlying tool executing exactly once.

**What was not tested**: Live cron/isolated-agent end-to-end spawn with a real `before_tool_call` plugin was not driven in this environment (requires a configured plugin + cron schedule + real provider).

**Repro confirmation**: The new "normalized already-wrapped tool" regression test replicates the exact `createOpenClawCodingTools` pipeline: wrap a tool → normalize it with `normalizeToolParameters` (which does `{...tool, parameters}`) → re-wrap with new context. Before the Symbol-preservation fix, `normalizeToolParameters` dropped the source-tool reference and the re-wrap fell through to a degraded path. After the fix, metadata survives and the re-wrap produces a fresh single wrapper with the new context.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Copying all Symbol-keyed properties in `normalizeToolParameters` may preserve Symbols from other subsystems that shouldn't be shared between tool instances.
  **Mitigation**: The Symbols on tool objects are metadata markers deliberately placed there by the wrapping infrastructure. `preserveToolMeta` already copies plugin and channel metadata for the same reason. If a future subsystem adds a Symbol that must NOT be copied, it should use a non-own Symbol (prototype) or clean up after normalization.

- **Risk**: Re-wrapping an already-wrapped tool discards the previous context in favor of the new one.
  **Mitigation**: This is the intended behavior — the caller explicitly passes a new `ctx`. Callers that need to preserve context should not call `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` again.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] agents
- [x] plugin-sdk (before_tool_call hook infrastructure)

### Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.schema.test.ts`

### Review Findings Addressed

- **[P1] Fix the normalized-wrapper path so metadata survives normalization and one wrapper is rebuilt with the current hook context**: ✅ Fixed — `normalizeToolParameters` now preserves all Symbol-keyed metadata via `Object.getOwnPropertySymbols` + `Object.defineProperty`. The `BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_SOURCE_TOOL` reference survives object spread, so `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook` can extract the source tool and rebuild exactly one wrapper with the current context.
- **[P1] Add regression coverage that normalizes an already wrapped tool before rewrapping and asserts one hook call with the new context**: ✅ Added — new test "re-wraps a normalized already-wrapped tool with the current context (createOpenClawCodingTools path)" replicates the full pipeline: wrap → normalize (`{...tool, parameters}`) → re-wrap → execute once.
- **[P1] Add redacted live cron or isolated-agent plugin-hook output**: Live cron/isolated-agent end-to-end requires a configured plugin + cron schedule + real provider, unavailable in this headless Linux environment. The normalized-wrapper regression test proves the code-level behavior for the exact pipeline described in the issue.

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #92973

target.title: fix(memory-core): exclude archive transcripts from dreaming session corpus collection
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Dreaming session corpus ingestion used `listSessionFilesForAgent` which returns usage-counted files including `.deleted` and `.reset` archive transcripts. This caused archive transcripts to be re-ingested as duplicates alongside the current primary transcript, polluting the dreaming corpus with up to 91% automation noise on cron-heavy deployments.
- **Fix**: Skip archive artifacts in the dreaming session corpus collection loop via `isSessionArchiveArtifactName`, mirroring the existing checkpoint skip pattern. No change to `listSessionFilesForAgent` or any shared helper — memory_search archive indexing is unaffected.
- **Scope**: Only the dreaming corpus collection path in `collectSessionIngestionBatches`. Light/REM phase logic, narrative writer, and all other dreaming paths are unchanged.

## Linked context

Related #90313

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Archived transcripts (`.deleted.*`, `.reset.*`) no longer pollute dreaming session corpus during light dreaming ingestion.

**Real environment tested:** Node v22 on Linux (WSL2), branch `fix/issue-90313-dreaming-corpus-archive-filter`, head `29b6db19b7a`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_CONFIG=test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts \
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts
```

This runs the regression `excludes archive (.deleted/.reset) transcripts from session corpus collection`, which exercises the patched production path end-to-end:

`beforeAgentReply` → `runPhaseIfTriggered` (light) → `runLightDreaming` → `ingestSessionTranscriptSignals` → `collectSessionIngestionBatches` (archive skip at `dreaming-phases.ts:863-865`).

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
 RUN  v4.1.7 /home/princeht/fire_project/openclaw

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
   Duration  8.51s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 14.28s
```

Session corpus written by the light dreaming sweep (`memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-04-16.txt`) contains primary transcript lines and excludes archive-only content:

```text
User: Document the memory-core plugin setup.
Assistant: I documented the memory-core plugin setup.
```

Archive-only strings verified absent from corpus:

```text
Archive message that must not appear.
I replied to the archived session.
Reset-archive content that must be excluded.
```

**Observed result after fix:** After a patched light dreaming sweep over primary + `.deleted` + `.reset` transcripts, only the primary transcript content is ingested into the session corpus; archive transcript bodies are filtered out before `appendSessionCorpusLines`.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway heartbeat on a production OpenClaw deployment with real cron subagent spawns.

## Tests and validation

- Regression test added: `excludes archive (.deleted/.reset) transcripts from session corpus collection` — creates primary + `.deleted` + `.reset` transcripts, runs light dreaming, asserts archive content absent from corpus while primary content remains
- Existing test `drops archive, cron, and heartbeat chatter from fresh session corpus output` continues to pass

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes — dreaming corpus will no longer contain content from archive transcripts; dream quality should improve on cron-heavy deployments
- Config, environment, or migration change: No
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution change: No
- Highest-risk area: Low — the change is purely subtractive (removes files from ingestion, never adds)
- No data is deleted; archive files remain on disk and remain indexed by memory_search

## Current review state

Next action: `@clawsweeper re-review` on head `29b6db19b7a`.
Waiting on: proof label refresh after re-review.
Bot comments addressed: PR body now documents patched light-dreaming corpus collection proof (not just the filename predicate).

Related #90313

target.title: fix(sessions): persist sender metadata in user turn transcript JSONL
target.body:
## Summary

- Thread `senderId`/`senderName`/`senderUsername` from the channel inbound context into the persisted user-turn transcript message
- Group chat session JSONL records now include `__openclaw` sender identity metadata, matching the metadata already present in direct (1:1) session records
- When no sender metadata is available (empty/null values), the `__openclaw` block is omitted — preserving existing behavior
- `senderE164` (phone number) is intentionally excluded from the persisted fields for privacy

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / [ ] Feature / [ ] Refactor / [ ] Docs / [ ] Security / [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / [ ] Skills / [ ] Auth / [ ] Memory / [ ] Integrations / [ ] API / [ ] UI/UX / [x] Sessions / [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Group chat user messages persisted to the session JSONL transcript were missing `__openclaw` sender identity metadata (`senderId`, `senderName`, etc.) that is present in direct (1:1) session records.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, pnpm test + production function via `node --import tsx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Production `appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage` with sender metadata:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage } from './src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.ts';
import fs from 'node:fs';
import os from 'node:os';
import path from 'node:path';

const dir = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-proof-'));
const transcriptPath = path.join(dir, 'session.jsonl');

await appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage({
  transcriptPath, sessionId: 'session-proof', sessionKey: 'proof', cwd: dir,
  input: {
    text: 'hello from group chat',
    senderId: '8489979671',
    senderName: 'Ram Shenoy',
    senderUsername: 'ram_s',
  },
  updateMode: 'none',
});

const rawLines = fs.readFileSync(transcriptPath, 'utf-8').trim().split('\n');
const msg = JSON.parse(rawLines.filter(l => l.trim()).slice(-1)[0]).message;
console.log('Has __openclaw:', '__openclaw' in msg);
console.log('__openclaw:', JSON.stringify(msg.__openclaw));
"
```

Regression tests:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
Has __openclaw: true
__openclaw: {"senderId":"8489979671","senderName":"Ram Shenoy","senderUsername":"ram_s"}

No-sender case - has __openclaw: false

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  27 passed (27)
```

**Observed result after the fix:** Group chat user turn messages persisted via the production `appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage` now include `__openclaw` sender identity metadata in the JSONL output. Empty/null sender fields omit the envelope.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway session with real Telegram group chat message delivery.

## Root Cause

The shared `UserTurnInput` → `buildPersistedUserTurnMessage` path only persisted text, media, and provenance. Sender identity from the channel inbound context (`SenderId`, `SenderName`, `SenderUsername`) was carried into the agent runtime but not threaded into the user-turn transcript message written to the session JSONL.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target: `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts`
- Two new test cases: one asserting `__openclaw` envelope with sender metadata, one asserting `__openclaw` is omitted when no sender metadata is provided
- All 27 tests pass

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes — group chat JSONL transcripts now include optional `__openclaw` sender metadata when channel context provides it
- Config, environment, or migration change: No
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution change: No — only public channel identifiers (`senderId`, `senderName`, `senderUsername`) are persisted; `senderE164` is explicitly excluded for privacy; no new auth/credential/sandbox surface
- Highest-risk area: Persisted session transcript metadata (security-boundary / sensitive-data)
- Mitigation: Omit envelope when all sender fields are empty; exclude phone numbers; fields mirror existing 1:1 session metadata contract; no new config/env knobs; regression tests cover both present and absent sender cases

## Current review state

Next action: `@clawsweeper re-review` after security-boundary risk checklist and proof refresh
Waiting on: `proof: sufficient`, `rating: 🦞 diamond lobster`
Bot comments addressed: removed `senderE164` from persisted envelope per privacy feedback

Ref #90531

target.title: fix(agents): apply stale-run liveness check to aborted subagent orphan recovery
target.body:
## Summary

Fix #90766: stale subagent runs (>2h) with `abortedLastRun: true` were exempt from stale-unended pruning, causing very old aborted child sessions to be resurrected after long gateway downtime.

Changes:
- **`subagent-registry-helpers.ts`**: Remove the `abortedLastRun` exemption from `resolveSubagentRunOrphanReason` — stale aborted runs are now classified as `stale-unended-run` like any other stale run
- **`subagent-orphan-recovery.ts`**: Add `isStaleUnendedSubagentRun` check before attempting recovery; await `finalizeInterruptedSubagentRun` and report `failedRuns` when finalization does not update the registry (scheduler retry path)
- **`subagent-orphan-recovery.test.ts`**: Add regression coverage for stale finalization success and finalization-failure accounting

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Before: `resolveSubagentRunOrphanReason` skipped stale-unended classification when `abortedLastRun === true`. `recoverOrphanedSubagentSessions` had no age gate and fire-and-forget finalization could leave stale runs unended without scheduler retry. After: stale aborted runs are pruned at registry restore and finalized during orphan recovery; fresh aborted runs still recover.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production functions via `node --import tsx` and focused Vitest.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Production liveness helper (stale vs fresh cutoff):

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { isStaleUnendedSubagentRun } from './src/agents/subagent-run-liveness.ts';
const now = Date.now();
const TWO_HOURS = 2 * 60 * 60 * 1000;
const stale = { createdAt: now - 3 * TWO_HOURS, startedAt: now - 3 * TWO_HOURS };
const fresh = { createdAt: now - 30 * 60 * 1000, startedAt: now - 30 * 60 * 1000 };
console.log('stale aborted (3h):', isStaleUnendedSubagentRun(stale, now));
console.log('fresh aborted (30m):', isStaleUnendedSubagentRun(fresh, now));
"
```

Orphan recovery + persistence regression:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-orphan-recovery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.persistence.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
stale aborted (3h): true
fresh aborted (30m): false

Test Files  2 passed (2)
     Tests  39 passed (39)
```

New orphan-recovery tests confirm:
- stale aborted run (`startedAt` 3h ago) calls `finalizeInterruptedSubagentRun`, does not resume via gateway
- finalization returning `0` increments `failed` and populates `failedRuns` for scheduler retry
- fresh aborted run (`startedAt` 55s ago) still resumes normally (existing test)

**Observed result after the fix:** Stale aborted subagent runs (>2h, no `endedAt`) are finalized instead of resurrected after gateway restart. Fresh aborted runs (<2h) continue through orphan recovery. Finalization failures are accounted in `failedRuns` so the scheduler retry path can recover.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway with multi-hour downtime and real LLM-backed subagents (default stale window is 2h).

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes — very old aborted subagent runs (>2h) will be finalized instead of resurrected after gateway restart
- Config, environment, or migration change: No
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution change: No
- Highest-risk area: Session-state cutoff for restart-aborted child sessions older than the stale window; fresh aborted runs are unchanged
- Mitigation: Shared `isStaleUnendedSubagentRun` policy used at both registry restore and orphan recovery; await finalization + `failedRuns` accounting; persistence + orphan-recovery regression tests (39/39 pass)

## Current review state

Next action: `@clawsweeper re-review` after P2 finalization-accounting fix and expanded proof
Waiting on: `proof: sufficient`, `rating: 🦞 diamond lobster`
Bot comments addressed: P2 await-finalization finding fixed in `8ebc547a5e8`

Fixes: #90766
